1:
On 1 Corinthians 4:1 — “Let a man so account of us, as of the ministers of Christ, and stewards of the mysteries of God” — the witnesses mark the true estimate of ministers.

The grammatical-historical commentators define the two titles. Robertson: “ministers” (hyperetas) are “under-rowers,” subordinate servants of Christ. Wesley: the word “signifies such servants as laboured at the oar… the pains which every faithful minister takes.” Ellicott: it “expresses more strongly the idea of subordination.” The Puritan Henry: ministers are to be valued, “though… not… over-valued.”

The verse fixes the right estimate of the apostles. Regard us, Paul says, as servants of Christ and stewards entrusted with God’s mysteries — neither despised nor idolised, but valued as what we are: subordinate servants and trusted stewards.

So the witnesses see Paul set the proper valuation of ministers between two errors. The witnesses note the vivid first title — “under-rowers,” the lowest servants who pull at the oar — which marks the minister’s subordination and toil under Christ his Master. The second, “stewards of the mysteries of God,” raises the dignity: they are entrusted with the household treasures of the gospel, to dispense them faithfully. Both titles correct the Corinthians’ party-spirit: ministers are not masters to be followed as heads of sects, nor mere men to be despised, but Christ’s servants and God’s stewards. They mark Henry’s balance — valued, not over-valued. The verse thus gives the church the right way to regard its teachers: as servants and stewards, accountable to Christ and God, not to be enrolled under as party-leaders. It prepares the central point of the next verses — that the one thing required of a steward is faithfulness, and the one judge who matters is the Lord.

So the witnesses see Paul set the proper valuation of ministers between two errors. The vivid first title — “under-rowers,” the lowest servants who pull at the oar — marks the minister’s subordination and toil under Christ. The second, “stewards of the mysteries of God,” raises the dignity: they are entrusted with the treasures of the gospel. Both correct the party-spirit: ministers are not masters to be followed as heads of sects, nor mere men to be despised, but Christ’s servants and God’s stewards — valued, not over-valued. It prepares the central point: that the one thing required of a steward is faithfulness, and the one judge who matters is the Lord.
2:
On 1 Corinthians 4:2 — “Moreover it is required in stewards, that a man be found faithful” — the witnesses mark the one requirement of a steward.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the demand. Ellicott: “here (on earth) inquiry is made in the case of stewards… that one is faithful,” “found” meaning “proved to be.” Robertson: “it is sought” (zeteitai). The Reformed Calvin: “it is not enough to be a steward, if there be not an upright stewardship… the rule… is… fidelity.” Barnes: the apostles acted “from a higher principle than a desire to please man.”

The verse names the single thing required of a steward: faithfulness. Whatever his gifts or success, the steward’s one obligation is to be faithful to his master’s trust. By this he is to be judged.

So the witnesses see Paul fix the standard by which ministers are to be measured. The witnesses note that of a steward only one thing is required — not brilliance, eloquence, or popularity, but fidelity to the trust committed. The steward dispenses his master’s goods, not his own; his duty is to be faithful in that dispensing. They mark Calvin’s point that the title of steward is empty without an upright stewardship, whose rule is faithfulness. This both rebukes the Corinthians, who valued teachers by their wisdom and rhetoric, and frees the minister from the tyranny of human opinion: he answers to his Master for his faithfulness, not to the crowd for his gifts. The verse thus establishes the true measure of ministry — faithfulness to God’s trust — and prepares Paul’s declaration that he is little concerned with men’s judgment, since the only verdict that counts is the Lord’s, who will reckon with his stewards in his own day.

So the witnesses see Paul fix the standard by which ministers are measured. Of a steward only one thing is required — not brilliance, eloquence, or popularity, but fidelity to the trust committed. The steward dispenses his master’s goods, not his own; his duty is to be faithful in that dispensing. This both rebukes the Corinthians, who valued teachers by wisdom and rhetoric, and frees the minister from the tyranny of human opinion: he answers to his Master for his faithfulness, not to the crowd for his gifts. The true measure of ministry is faithfulness to God’s trust.
3:
On 1 Corinthians 4:3 — “But with me it is a very small thing that I should be judged of you, or of man’s judgment: yea, I judge not mine own self” — the witnesses mark Paul’s indifference to human judgment.

The Eastern fathers note the theme. Chrysostom: against “the disease of restless prying and… unseasonable curiosity, which Christ… chastised” (Matthew 7:1). The grammatical-historical Robertson: “with me” (ethical dative), “a very small thing” (eis elachiston). Barnes: Paul was not insensible to good opinion, but esteemed men’s judgment of small concern, being “responsible… to my Master only.”

The verse expresses Paul’s settled indifference to being judged by men. Their verdict on him, or any human tribunal’s, weighs very little; he does not even rely on his own self-assessment. One judgment alone matters.

So the witnesses see Paul rise above the censures and praises of men. The witnesses note that, as a steward answerable to his Master, Paul counts the Corinthians’ judgment — and indeed any human court’s — a very small thing. He is not indifferent to a good conscience or careless of others’ good opinion, but he refuses to make men’s estimate the measure of his fidelity. They mark the further step: he does not even rest on his own judgment of himself, knowing how partial self-assessment is. This frees the minister from both the flattery and the criticism of partisans, and sets his eye on the Lord alone. The verse thus rebukes the Corinthians’ presumptuous habit of sitting in judgment on their teachers, and models the servant’s true posture: to do his work faithfully and leave the verdict to God. Human courts, and even self, are incompetent judges of the heart; the Lord alone can rightly try the steward.

So the witnesses see Paul rise above the censures and praises of men. As a steward answerable to his Master, he counts the Corinthians’ judgment — and any human court’s — a very small thing. He is not careless of a good conscience or others’ opinion, but refuses to make men’s estimate the measure of his fidelity. He does not even rest on his own self-judgment, knowing how partial it is. This frees the minister from both the flattery and the criticism of partisans, and sets his eye on the Lord alone — rebuking the Corinthians’ presumptuous habit of sitting in judgment on their teachers.
4:
On 1 Corinthians 4:4 — “For I know nothing by myself; yet am I not hereby justified: but he that judgeth me is the Lord” — the witnesses mark the limits of self-acquittal.

The grammatical-historical commentators clarify the scope. Robertson: “I know nothing against myself” (ouden synoida), not a claim of sinlessness but an hypothesis showing “the unreliability of mere… self-satisfaction.” Calvin and Barnes: this refers to “the office of apostleship,” not his whole life. Wesley: “I depend not on this as a sufficient justification… he that judgeth me is the Lord.”

The verse measures the worth of a clear conscience. Even if Paul knows nothing against himself in his stewardship, that does not acquit him; the Lord is his judge. A good conscience is not the final verdict.

So the witnesses see Paul deny that even a clear conscience is the ultimate court. The witnesses are careful, with Calvin, to note the scope: Paul speaks of his faithfulness as an apostle, not of sinlessness in general — elsewhere he confesses his indwelling sin. Even within his office, though he knows of no unfaithfulness, he will not on that ground pronounce himself justified; for self-knowledge is fallible and the heart deceitful. The decisive judgment belongs to the Lord, who searches what conscience cannot fully see. They mark how this both humbles the minister — not even his own approval suffices — and exalts the one true Judge. The verse thus completes the lesson on judgment: men’s opinion counts for little, one’s own for not enough; the Lord alone is competent to try the steward. It teaches a holy reserve about self-assessment and a final reliance on God’s righteous judgment, which will one day make all things plain.

So the witnesses see Paul deny that even a clear conscience is the ultimate court. They are careful, with Calvin, to note the scope: he speaks of his faithfulness as an apostle, not of sinlessness. Even within his office, though he knows of no unfaithfulness, he will not on that ground pronounce himself justified; for self-knowledge is fallible. The decisive judgment belongs to the Lord, who searches what conscience cannot fully see. This both humbles the minister — not even his own approval suffices — and exalts the one true Judge, teaching a holy reserve about self-assessment and a final reliance on God’s righteous judgment.
5:
On 1 Corinthians 4:5 — “Therefore judge nothing before the time, until the Lord come, who will… make manifest the counsels of the hearts: and then shall every man have praise of God” — the witnesses mark the warning against premature judgment.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the time and the manifestation. Robertson: “stop passing judgment” (me ti krinete) — “the censorious habit was ruining the Corinthian Church” (Matthew 7:1); “before the time,” the day of the Lord. Barnes: not to “pass a harsh” judgment, for God “shall reveal the secrets of all hearts.” Calvin: he reproves “hasty and rash” judgment, not all judgment.

The verse forbids premature judgment and points to the great day. Judge nothing before its time; wait for the Lord, who will bring the hidden things to light and lay bare the heart’s counsels — and then each will have his praise from God.

So the witnesses see Paul check the Corinthians’ censorious habit by appeal to the coming judgment. The witnesses note that he forbids not all discernment but hasty, rash, premature verdicts — the sitting in judgment on others’ hearts and motives, which only God can read. The reason is the certain coming of the Lord, who will illuminate what darkness now hides and disclose the inner counsels of every heart. Until then, judgment is out of season. They mark the gracious turn at the end: “then shall every man have praise of God” — the faithful steward, perhaps now misjudged by men, will receive his commendation from God, who alone judges truly. The verse thus crowns the section on judgment: leave the verdict to the Lord’s day, when motives will be manifest and praise rightly given. It rebukes the Corinthians’ party-judgments, comforts the misjudged servant, and turns all eyes from the tribunal of men to the righteous judgment of God.

So the witnesses see Paul check the Corinthians’ censorious habit by appeal to the coming judgment. He forbids not all discernment but hasty, premature verdicts — the sitting in judgment on others’ hearts, which only God can read. The reason is the certain coming of the Lord, who will illuminate what darkness now hides. They mark the gracious turn at the end: “then shall every man have praise of God” — the faithful steward, now perhaps misjudged, will receive his commendation from God, who alone judges truly. It rebukes the party-judgments, comforts the misjudged servant, and turns all eyes to the righteous judgment of God.
6:
On 1 Corinthians 4:6 — “And these things… I have in a figure transferred to myself and to Apollos for your sakes; that ye might learn in us not to think of men above that which is written” — the witnesses mark Paul’s tactful method.

The Eastern fathers note the strategy. Chrysostom: Paul argued “as if he were himself the person… addressed,” that the dignity of the named persons might restrain wrath. The grammatical-historical Robertson: “transferred” (meteschematisa), to change the form, applying to himself and Apollos what concerned the real party-leaders. Ellicott: he named only himself and Apollos “so that his motive… may not be misunderstood.” Wesley: “not to think… above that which is written.”

The verse discloses Paul’s gentle device. He has applied to himself and Apollos what really concerned the factious teachers, for the Corinthians’ sake — that they might learn, in this safe example, not to esteem any man beyond Scripture’s warrant.

So the witnesses see the pastoral tact behind Paul’s whole argument. The witnesses note that, rather than name and inflame the actual heads of the factions, Paul cast the discussion in terms of himself and Apollos — figures the Corinthians revered and would not resent — so that the lesson might be received without offence. This was love’s strategy, “for your sakes.” The aim was that they learn “not to think of men above that which is written”: to keep their estimate of teachers within the bounds of Scripture, neither idolising nor dividing over them. They mark Chrysostom’s insight that the dignity of the named persons disarmed the rebuke. The verse thus reveals the method of the foregoing chapters and states their purpose: to cure the Corinthians’ man-centred pride by a gentle, indirect example, and to bind their judgments of ministers to the rule of God’s word, so that none be exalted beyond what is written — and none be puffed up, as the next words warn.

So the witnesses see the pastoral tact behind Paul’s whole argument. Rather than name and inflame the actual heads of the factions, he cast the discussion in terms of himself and Apollos — figures the Corinthians revered and would not resent — so the lesson might be received without offence. The aim was that they learn “not to think of men above that which is written”: to keep their estimate of teachers within Scripture’s bounds. They mark Chrysostom’s insight that the dignity of the named persons disarmed the rebuke. The method binds their judgments of ministers to the rule of God’s word, that none be exalted beyond what is written.
7:
On 1 Corinthians 4:7 — “For who maketh thee to differ from another? and what hast thou that thou didst not receive? now if thou didst receive it, why dost thou glory, as if thou hadst not received it?” — the witnesses mark the cure for pride.

The grammatical-historical commentators press the three questions. Robertson: “maketh thee to differ” (diakrinei), distinguishes thee — “all self-conceit rests on the notion of superiority… as if self-bestowed.” The Puritan Henry: “all the distinction… was owing to God.” Wesley: “as if thou hadst it originally from thyself.” Ellicott: “it is the free gift of God.”

The verse demolishes pride with three questions. Who made you different? What do you have that you did not receive? And if you received it, why boast as if you had not? Every advantage is a gift; boasting is therefore absurd.

So the witnesses see Paul lay the axe to the root of self-conceit. The witnesses note the relentless logic of the three questions: every distinction a man has — gift, grace, knowledge — was conferred, not self-produced; it was received from God; therefore to glory in it as one’s own is folly and ingratitude. The whole edifice of pride rests on the false notion that one’s superiority is self-bestowed; Paul demolishes it by reminding the Corinthians that all they have is a free gift. They mark Henry’s point that all that distinguished them was owing to God. This single verse, the witnesses observe, is the sovereign remedy for the boasting that fed the factions: if all is received, none may boast. It humbles the gifted teacher and the partisan follower alike, turning every ground of glorying into a cause of thanksgiving. The verse thus answers the Corinthians’ pride at its source — and prepares the biting irony with which Paul will expose their self-satisfied conceit in the verses that follow.

So the witnesses see Paul lay the axe to the root of self-conceit. The relentless logic of the three questions: every distinction a man has was conferred, not self-produced; it was received from God; therefore to glory in it as one’s own is folly and ingratitude. The whole edifice of pride rests on the false notion that one’s superiority is self-bestowed. This single verse is the sovereign remedy for the boasting that fed the factions: if all is received, none may boast. It humbles the gifted teacher and the partisan follower alike, turning every ground of glorying into a cause of thanksgiving.
8:
On 1 Corinthians 4:8 — “Now ye are full, now ye are rich, ye have reigned as kings without us: and I would to God ye did reign, that we also might reign with you” — the witnesses mark the irony against complacency.

The grammatical-historical commentators read the sarcasm. Robertson: “already are ye filled?” (kekoresmenoi) — “keen irony, even sarcasm.” Ellicott: “these… sentences are ironical… as if you had already attained the crowning point.” Chrysostom (in Ellicott): “piety is an insatiable thing.” Wesley: the Corinthians, “suffering nothing… were pleased with… themselves.”

The verse turns to stinging irony. Already full, already rich, reigning like kings — and all without the apostles! Paul mocks their self-satisfied sense of having arrived, then adds the wish that they truly did reign, so the apostles might share it.

So the witnesses see Paul expose the Corinthians’ complacency by biting irony. The witnesses note the three sarcastic strokes: they are full, rich, royal — as if they had already reached the summit of the Christian life, needing nothing further, and that without the apostles who founded them. The irony cuts at their self-conceit: a church still carnal and divided fancied itself perfected and reigning. They mark the touching turn in the wish “I would to God ye did reign” — Paul would rejoice if their boast were true, for then the apostles too would share the glory; but the reign is not yet, the kingdom not yet come. The verse thus contrasts the Corinthians’ premature triumphalism with the suffering reality of the apostles, which the next verses will paint. It rebukes the smugness that supposed itself rich while the apostles were poor, reigning while they were despised — a self-flattery that betrayed how little they understood the cross-shaped path of true discipleship.

So the witnesses see Paul expose the Corinthians’ complacency by biting irony. The three sarcastic strokes — full, rich, royal, and that without the apostles — cut at their self-conceit: a church still carnal and divided fancied itself perfected and reigning. They mark the touching turn in the wish “I would to God ye did reign” — Paul would rejoice if their boast were true, for then the apostles too would share the glory; but the reign is not yet. The verse contrasts the Corinthians’ premature triumphalism with the suffering reality of the apostles, which the next verses will paint.
9:
On 1 Corinthians 4:9 — “For I think that God hath set forth us the apostles last, as it were appointed to death: for we are made a spectacle unto the world, and to angels, and to men” — the witnesses mark the apostles as a spectacle.

The grammatical-historical commentators draw on the arena. Clarke and Ellicott: the imagery is of the Roman games — the apostles like the bestiarii or condemned gladiators “brought out last… appointed to death.” Robertson: “set forth… last” (eschatous apedeixen), a gladiatorial show; “spectacle” (theatron). Wesley: “brought forth last on the stage… devoted to death.”

The verse drops the irony for a vivid picture. God has set the apostles last, like men condemned to die in the arena, a spectacle for the whole universe — world, angels, and men — to gaze upon. Their lot is the opposite of the Corinthians’ fancied glory.

So the witnesses see Paul paint the apostles’ true condition in the imagery of the amphitheatre. The witnesses note the arresting figure: as the doomed combatants were brought out last to die before the crowd, so God has exhibited the apostles, “appointed to death,” a public spectacle to the whole creation — the watching world, the angels above, and all men. Far from reigning as kings, they are paraded as condemned men. They mark the universal audience: the apostles’ sufferings are a theatre beheld by heaven and earth, a display of the cross-shaped life. The verse thus sets the suffering reality of true apostleship against the Corinthians’ self-satisfied ease, shaming their pretended royalty. The men they should have honoured were treated by the world as the lowest of the low, yet their very abasement was a spectacle of faithfulness that angels and men beheld. It is the answer to the irony of verse 8: the apostles reign not now but suffer, last and least in the world’s eyes, awaiting their crown.

So the witnesses see Paul paint the apostles’ true condition in the imagery of the amphitheatre. As the doomed combatants were brought out last to die before the crowd, so God has exhibited the apostles, “appointed to death,” a public spectacle to the whole creation — world, angels, and men. Far from reigning as kings, they are paraded as condemned men. The verse sets the suffering reality of true apostleship against the Corinthians’ self-satisfied ease: the men they should have honoured were treated by the world as the lowest of the low, yet their very abasement was a spectacle of faithfulness that angels and men beheld.
10:
On 1 Corinthians 4:10 — “We are fools for Christ’s sake, but ye are wise in Christ; we are weak, but ye are strong; ye are honourable, but we are despised” — the witnesses mark the threefold ironic contrast.

The Eastern fathers note the sharpness. Chrysostom: having said they reign while the apostles are doomed, he shows how, naming “fools… weak… despised.” The grammatical-historical Robertson: “we—you… triple contrast in keenest ironical emphasis” — teaching, demeanour, worldly position; the apostles “fools for Christ’s sake” (Philippians 3:7). Ellicott: “charged with irony.”

The verse drives the irony home in three antitheses. We apostles are fools, weak, despised; you Corinthians are wise, strong, honourable. The contrast exposes how absurdly the church exalts itself above its suffering founders.

So the witnesses see Paul sharpen the contrast between the suffering apostles and the self-exalting church. The witnesses note the three pointed antitheses — fools versus wise, weak versus strong, despised versus honourable — touching, as Robertson observes, teaching, demeanour, and worldly standing. The irony is biting: the apostles, who bear the reproach of the cross, are counted fools and weaklings by the world; the Corinthians, who shun such shame, fancy themselves wise and strong “in Christ.” They mark that the apostles’ folly is “for Christ’s sake” — a chosen reproach, the badge of fidelity. The verse thus lays bare the Corinthians’ inverted values: they prized the very things — wisdom, strength, honour — that the cross calls into question, and despised the reproach that the apostles gloried in. It is a mirror held up to a church ashamed of the cross, contrasting its comfortable self-esteem with the apostolic willingness to be fools and outcasts for Christ — and preparing the catalogue of real sufferings that follows.

So the witnesses see Paul sharpen the contrast between the suffering apostles and the self-exalting church. The three pointed antitheses — fools versus wise, weak versus strong, despised versus honourable — touch teaching, demeanour, and worldly standing. The apostles, who bear the reproach of the cross, are counted fools by the world; the Corinthians, who shun such shame, fancy themselves wise and strong “in Christ.” The apostles’ folly is “for Christ’s sake” — a chosen reproach, the badge of fidelity. The verse is a mirror held up to a church ashamed of the cross, prizing the very things the cross calls into question.
11:
On 1 Corinthians 4:11 — “Even unto this present hour we both hunger, and thirst, and are naked, and are buffeted, and have no certain dwellingplace” — the witnesses mark the catalogue of hardship.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the present reality. Barnes: Paul “drops the irony… the sufferings had been incessant… not merely at the commencement.” Robertson: “this very minute” (achri tes arti horas) — ten verbs and four participles give “a graphic picture of Paul’s condition in Ephesus.” Ellicott: the sad description shows “how intensely true that last word ‘despised’ was.”

The verse turns from irony to plain reality. To this very hour the apostles hunger, thirst, go ill-clad, are beaten, and have no settled home. The contrast with the Corinthians’ ease is no figure of speech but Paul’s daily lot.

So the witnesses see Paul lay bare the actual hardships of apostolic life. The witnesses note that the irony now gives way to sober fact: even as he writes, Paul endures hunger, thirst, nakedness, blows, and homelessness — the very opposite of the Corinthians’ full and reigning state. The phrase “to this present hour” presses the point: these are not past trials of his early ministry but his continuing condition. They mark the vividness of the catalogue, a living picture of the cost of faithful service. The verse thus grounds the foregoing contrast in undeniable reality: the despised apostle is not a rhetorical pose but a hungry, beaten, homeless man, while the church he founded sits in comfort and pride. It shames the Corinthians’ complacency by the spectacle of their father’s sufferings, and it dignifies the hardships of gospel ministry as the mark of fidelity to a crucified Lord — a fellowship in his reproach that the next verses will extend to the apostles’ gracious response under it.

So the witnesses see Paul lay bare the actual hardships of apostolic life. The irony now gives way to sober fact: even as he writes, Paul endures hunger, thirst, nakedness, blows, and homelessness — the very opposite of the Corinthians’ full and reigning state. The phrase “to this present hour” presses the point: these are not past trials but his continuing condition. It shames the Corinthians’ complacency by the spectacle of their father’s sufferings, and dignifies the hardships of gospel ministry as the mark of fidelity to a crucified Lord — a fellowship in his reproach.
12:
On 1 Corinthians 4:12 — “And labour, working with our own hands: being reviled, we bless; being persecuted, we suffer it” — the witnesses mark the apostles’ toil and gracious response.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the manual labour. Robertson: “we toil” (kopiomen), working with our own hands (Acts 18:3; 1 Thessalonians 2:9) — tent-making with Aquila and Priscilla. Clarke: they laboured to supply themselves where no church was formed. Wesley: “we do not return revilings… nothing but blessing.”

The verse adds toil and Christlike forbearance to the catalogue. The apostles labour with their own hands; when reviled, they bless; when persecuted, they endure. They meet abuse not with retaliation but with grace.

So the witnesses see the apostles’ hardship crowned by their Christlike response. The witnesses note the manual labour — Paul working at tent-making to support himself and not burden the churches — a further mark of the lowliness the Corinthians despised. But the deeper point is the spirit: reviled, they bless; persecuted, they bear it. This is the very temper of Christ, who when reviled, reviled not again. They mark how sharply this rebukes the Corinthians’ pride and contention: while the apostles toiled and blessed their abusers, the church quarrelled and exalted itself. The verse thus shows that the apostolic life is not only suffering but suffering met with grace — returning blessing for cursing, patience for persecution. It holds up the pattern of the cross not merely in hardship endured but in love returned for injury, the opposite of the carnal strife at Corinth. The despised apostle is also the gracious one, embodying in his own conduct the gospel he preached.

So the witnesses see the apostles’ hardship crowned by their Christlike response. The manual labour — Paul working at tent-making to support himself — is a further mark of the lowliness the Corinthians despised. But the deeper point is the spirit: reviled, they bless; persecuted, they bear it — the very temper of Christ, who when reviled, reviled not again. This sharply rebukes the Corinthians’ pride and contention: while the apostles toiled and blessed their abusers, the church quarrelled. The apostolic life is not only suffering but suffering met with grace, returning blessing for cursing — the pattern of the cross.
13:
On 1 Corinthians 4:13 — “Being defamed, we intreat: we are made as the filth of the world, and are the offscouring of all things unto this day” — the witnesses mark the depth of the apostles’ abasement.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the strong words. Robertson: “being defamed we intreat” — “Paul is opening his very heart”; “filth of the world” (perikatharmata), “sweepings, rinsings.” Ellicott and Calvin: the terms recall the heathen expiatory victims, the lowest wretches devoted, loaded with curses, as a cleansing for the city. Wesley: such were taken “from the dregs of the people, to be offered as expiatory sacrifices.”

The verse reaches the climax of abasement. Defamed, the apostles answer with entreaty; they are treated as the world’s refuse, the scum and offscouring of all things. No image of contempt could be lower.

So the witnesses see the apostles’ humiliation described in the most extreme terms. The witnesses note the gracious response continued — defamed, they entreat, answering slander with gentle appeal — and then the climactic image: they are made “the filth of the world… the offscouring of all things,” the very sweepings and rinsings, the refuse swept up and cast out. They mark the dark background the fathers and expositors supply: the words recall the wretches whom the heathen, in time of plague, loaded with curses and offered as expiatory victims to cleanse the city — the most despised of men, devoted to death for others. So the apostles were treated by the world: its scum, its scapegoats. The verse thus completes the portrait of apostolic suffering at its lowest point, and shames the Corinthians’ pride to the utmost: while they reigned in honour, their fathers in the faith were counted the offscouring of mankind. Yet in this abasement, borne with grace, lies the true glory of the cross — and the rebuke to a church ashamed of it.

So the witnesses see the apostles’ humiliation described in the most extreme terms. The gracious response continues — defamed, they entreat — and then the climactic image: they are made “the filth of the world… the offscouring of all things.” They mark the dark background the fathers supply: the words recall the wretches whom the heathen loaded with curses and offered as expiatory victims to cleanse a city. So the apostles were treated by the world: its scum, its scapegoats. While the Corinthians reigned in honour, their fathers in the faith were counted the offscouring of mankind — yet in this abasement, borne with grace, lies the true glory of the cross.
14:
On 1 Corinthians 4:14 — “I write not these things to shame you, but as my beloved sons I warn you” — the witnesses mark the turn to fatherly tenderness.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the change of tone. Robertson: “shaming you” (entrepon) — “the harsh tone has suddenly changed.” Ellicott: from “indignant expostulation… to… a tender and touching appeal.” The Puritan Henry: “not with the gall of an enemy, but the bowels of a father.” The Reformed Calvin: he had not spoken “with a view to cover them with shame, but… with paternal affection.”

The verse softens the rebuke with love. Paul has not written to shame the Corinthians but to warn them as his beloved children. The sharp irony was the discipline of a father, not the gall of an enemy.

So the witnesses see Paul reveal the loving heart behind his stinging words. The witnesses note the sudden, tender turn: having shamed their pride with irony and the catalogue of his sufferings, he hastens to assure them that his aim was not to humiliate but to admonish, as a father warns his dear children. The rebuke sprang from affection, not anger. They mark Henry’s lovely phrase — “not with the gall of an enemy, but the bowels of a father.” This guards the foregoing severity from being misread as contempt, and prepares the appeal to his unique fatherly relation to them. The verse thus models the spirit of right reproof: faithful enough to wound, loving enough to heal; the sharpness in the service of tenderness. Paul corrects them not to crush but to restore, as a father his children — and on the ground of that fatherhood, which he will now press, he can both warn them freely and win them back, for the rebukes of a father are the proofs of his love.

So the witnesses see Paul reveal the loving heart behind his stinging words. Having shamed their pride with irony and the catalogue of his sufferings, he hastens to assure them that his aim was not to humiliate but to admonish, as a father warns his dear children. The rebuke sprang from affection, not anger — “not with the gall of an enemy, but the bowels of a father” (Henry). This guards the foregoing severity from being misread as contempt, and models the spirit of right reproof: faithful enough to wound, loving enough to heal. Paul corrects them not to crush but to restore.
15:
On 1 Corinthians 4:15 — “For though ye have ten thousand instructers in Christ, yet have ye not many fathers: for in Christ Jesus I have begotten you through the gospel” — the witnesses mark Paul’s unique fatherhood.

The grammatical-historical commentators distinguish the roles. Robertson and Clarke: “instructers” (paidagogous) are the slave-attendants who led the child to school, “myriads” of them — but only one father, who “begot” them. Ellicott: “I, emphatic as opposed to many.” Cyril of Jerusalem cites this for the spiritual sense of “father” (Galatians 4:19).

The verse grounds Paul’s appeal in his unique relation. The Corinthians may have countless tutors, but only one father — Paul, who begot them in Christ through the gospel. The distinction gives him a claim no other teacher has.

So the witnesses see Paul press his singular spiritual fatherhood. The witnesses note the telling contrast: the Corinthians might have ten thousand paidagogoi — the guardian-slaves who escorted children and oversaw them, a common and replaceable office — but a father is another thing entirely, and of fathers they had but one. Paul alone begot them in Christ, founding the church and bringing them to new birth through the gospel. They mark the emphatic “I” set against the many. This both rebukes their running after other teachers and asserts his special, affectionate authority over them. They observe Cyril’s use of the verse for the legitimacy of spiritual fatherhood. The verse thus establishes the ground of Paul’s loving warning: as their father in the faith, he has a tenderness and a right that no mere instructor possesses. The relation is unique and unrepeatable — and from it flows the appeal of the next verse, that they imitate their father.

So the witnesses see Paul press his singular spiritual fatherhood. The Corinthians might have ten thousand paidagogoi — the guardian-slaves who escorted children, a common and replaceable office — but of fathers they had but one. Paul alone begot them in Christ, founding the church and bringing them to new birth through the gospel. The emphatic “I” is set against the many. This both rebukes their running after other teachers and asserts his special, affectionate authority. The relation is unique and unrepeatable — and from it flows the appeal that they imitate their father.
16:
On 1 Corinthians 4:16 — “Wherefore I beseech you, be ye followers of me” — the witnesses mark the call to imitation.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the word. Robertson: “be ye imitators of me” (mimetai mou ginesthe), “keep on becoming” imitators. Clarke: “imitators… as children should imitate their father.” Barnes: imitate especially his refusal to form parties. Ellicott: preserve “that family likeness” (Philippians 3:17).

The verse draws the practical appeal from the fatherhood. Since Paul is their father, he beseeches them to imitate him — to bear the family likeness, copying his spirit and conduct. The father’s example is the children’s pattern.

So the witnesses see Paul ground his exhortation in the natural duty of children to resemble their father. The witnesses note the gentleness of “I beseech” — he entreats rather than commands, though as a father he might command. The call is to ongoing imitation, a continual becoming like him; and in context, the witnesses observe, it is chiefly his humility, his refusal of party-spirit, his self-denying and Christlike temper that he holds up for copying. They mark Ellicott’s point that the appeal is to preserve the family likeness proper to children of such a father. This is no arrogance, for Paul imitates Christ and bids them follow as he follows the Lord; the pattern leads through him to Christ. The verse thus turns the doctrine into discipleship: let the Corinthians, who had divided over teachers, instead imitate their true father in his lowliness and love. The cure for their carnal pride is to become like the suffering, gracious, self-effacing apostle who begot them — and so, through him, like Christ.

So the witnesses see Paul ground his exhortation in the natural duty of children to resemble their father. The gentleness of “I beseech” — he entreats rather than commands, though as a father he might command. The call is to ongoing imitation, chiefly of his humility, his refusal of party-spirit, his self-denying and Christlike temper. This is no arrogance, for Paul imitates Christ and bids them follow as he follows the Lord; the pattern leads through him to Christ. The cure for their carnal pride is to become like the suffering, gracious, self-effacing apostle who begot them — and so, through him, like Christ.
17:
On 1 Corinthians 4:17 — “For this cause have I sent unto you Timotheus, who is my beloved son… who shall bring you into remembrance of my ways which be in Christ” — the witnesses mark the sending of Timothy.

The Eastern fathers note the love. Chrysostom: Paul, desirous to be present himself, sends Timothy, knowing “what a help to the disciples his presence was.” The grammatical-historical commentators detail the mission. Robertson: Timothy was sent ahead (Acts 19:21–22), “beloved son” in the faith; to remind them of Paul’s “ways… in Christ… as I teach every where.” Calvin: Timothy a faithful witness of Paul’s conduct.

The verse names the means of recall. Paul sends Timothy, his beloved and faithful son in the faith, to remind the Corinthians of his ways in Christ — the teaching and conduct he upholds in every church. The father sends a trusted son to refresh their memory.

So the witnesses see Paul reinforce his fatherly appeal by sending Timothy. The witnesses note the affection and aptness of the choice: Timothy, himself Paul’s “beloved son” in the faith and a faithful labourer, could both testify to and exemplify the apostle’s ways. His task was to bring the Corinthians “into remembrance” of Paul’s manner of life and teaching in Christ — the same that he taught consistently in every church, so that the Corinthians’ factions were a departure not only from Paul but from the universal practice of the gospel. They mark Chrysostom’s observation of Paul’s eagerness to help a divided church. The verse thus shows the father’s pastoral care in action: unable to come at once, he sends a trusted son to recall them to the apostolic pattern. It also quietly rebukes their novelty by appeal to what Paul taught “every where” — the common faith and order of all the churches, from which the Corinthians had strayed.

So the witnesses see Paul reinforce his fatherly appeal by sending Timothy. The affection and aptness of the choice: Timothy, himself Paul’s “beloved son” in the faith, could both testify to and exemplify the apostle’s ways. His task was to bring the Corinthians “into remembrance” of Paul’s teaching in Christ — the same he taught in every church, so that their factions were a departure from the universal practice of the gospel. They mark Chrysostom’s observation of Paul’s eagerness to help a divided church. It shows the father’s pastoral care in action, and quietly rebukes their novelty by appeal to what Paul taught everywhere.
18:
On 1 Corinthians 4:18 — “Now some are puffed up, as though I would not come to you” — the witnesses mark the arrogance of the absent-confident.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the conceit. Robertson: “some are puffed up” (ephysiothesan, the same swelling as in 3:6); “as though I were not coming.” Ellicott: the moment they heard Timothy was sent, they would say it proved Paul “afraid to come himself.” Calvin: “the custom of the false apostles — to take advantage of the absence of the good.”

The verse exposes a particular arrogance. Some at Corinth had grown puffed up, presuming that Paul would not come and so emboldened to vaunt themselves. They mistook his delay for fear.

So the witnesses see Paul confront the swollen confidence of the factious teachers. The witnesses note that some, taking advantage of his absence, had become inflated with self-importance, supposing the apostle would not return to call them to account — perhaps reading the sending of Timothy as a sign that Paul himself dared not come. They mark Calvin’s observation that this is ever the way of false teachers, who flourish while the faithful are away and presume on their continued absence. The verse thus identifies a specific manifestation of the Corinthian pride: an arrogance grown bold in the apostle’s absence, mistaking forbearance for weakness. It prepares Paul’s firm reply — that he will indeed come, and will then test not their swelling words but their actual power. The puffed-up presumption that he would stay away is about to be corrected by the promise of his coming, and the warning that he comes with apostolic authority to deal with their pretensions.

So the witnesses see Paul confront the swollen confidence of the factious teachers. Some, taking advantage of his absence, had become inflated with self-importance, supposing the apostle would not return to call them to account — perhaps reading the sending of Timothy as a sign that Paul dared not come. They mark Calvin’s observation that this is ever the way of false teachers, who flourish while the faithful are away. The verse identifies a specific manifestation of the Corinthian pride: an arrogance grown bold in the apostle’s absence, mistaking forbearance for weakness — about to be corrected by the promise of his coming.
19:
On 1 Corinthians 4:19 — “But I will come to you shortly, if the Lord will, and will know, not the speech of them which are puffed up, but the power” — the witnesses mark the promised reckoning.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the conditions and the test. Robertson: “if the Lord will” (ean ho kyrios thelese), the proper attitude (James 4:15); he will test “the power,” not the talk. Clarke: not their “eloquence… but their power — the authority they profess to have from God.” Calvin: not to terrify, but to repress their insolence.

The verse answers the presumption with a promise. Paul will come soon, if the Lord wills, and will put to the test not the fine words of the puffed-up but their actual power. Talk will not stand; reality will be tried.

So the witnesses see Paul promise to come and to test the pretenders. The witnesses note the pious reservation, “if the Lord will” — the apostle holds even his plans under God’s sovereign disposal, the right posture for all. They mark the sharp distinction he will draw: not the “speech” of the boasters — their eloquence and big claims — but their “power,” the real spiritual authority and effect, which alone proves a true minister. The puffed-up had much to say in his absence; he will measure them by what they can show. They observe Calvin’s point that Paul means not so much to terrify as to deflate their insolence. The verse thus answers the arrogance of verse 18 with apostolic firmness: Paul will come, God permitting, and the contest will not be of words but of power. It exposes the emptiness of mere talk and prepares the great principle of the next verse — that the kingdom of God consists not in word but in power.

So the witnesses see Paul promise to come and to test the pretenders. The pious reservation, “if the Lord will,” holds even his plans under God’s sovereign disposal. They mark the sharp distinction he will draw: not the “speech” of the boasters — their eloquence and big claims — but their “power,” the real spiritual authority that alone proves a true minister. The puffed-up had much to say in his absence; he will measure them by what they can show. Calvin notes that Paul means not so much to terrify as to deflate their insolence. The contest will not be of words but of power.
20:
On 1 Corinthians 4:20 — “For the kingdom of God is not in word, but in power” — the witnesses mark the great principle.

The grammatical-historical commentators state the truth. Barnes: the “kingdom of God… the power… in the government and discipline of the church” (Matthew 3:2); not “empty boasting… pompous pretensions.” Clarke: religion “not in word… but in power, the mighty energy of the Holy Spirit.” Wesley: “real religion does not consist in words, but in the power of God ruling the heart.”

The verse states the rule that governs the coming test. The kingdom of God consists not in talk but in power. Words, however eloquent, are nothing; the reality is the power of God at work.

So the witnesses see Paul lay down a principle that pierces to the heart of the Corinthian error. The witnesses note its breadth: the kingdom of God — whether in the church’s government, in true ministry, or in real religion itself — is a matter not of word but of power. The puffed-up teachers had abundance of speech; but eloquence and big claims are not the kingdom. What proves the genuine work of God is power: the mighty energy of the Spirit converting, sanctifying, ruling the heart, building the church. They mark Wesley’s plain summary — real religion is the power of God ruling the heart, not fine words. This is why Paul will test the boasters by their power, not their speech: only power reveals the presence of the kingdom. The verse thus exposes the hollowness of the Corinthians’ word-centred wisdom and party-rhetoric, and sets the true criterion for the church of every age: not what is said, but what God’s power effects. The kingdom is real, not verbal.

So the witnesses see Paul lay down a principle that pierces to the heart of the Corinthian error. The kingdom of God — whether in the church’s government, in true ministry, or in real religion itself — is a matter not of word but of power. The puffed-up teachers had abundance of speech; but eloquence and big claims are not the kingdom. What proves the genuine work of God is power: the mighty energy of the Spirit converting, sanctifying, ruling the heart. Wesley’s plain summary — real religion is the power of God ruling the heart, not fine words. The kingdom is real, not verbal.
21:
On 1 Corinthians 4:21 — “What will ye? shall I come unto you with a rod, or in love, and in the spirit of meekness?” — the witnesses mark the father’s choice put to the children.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the alternatives. Robertson: “with a rod” (en rhabdo), the shepherd’s or the schoolmaster’s staff; Paul “gives them the choice” — spiritual father or paedagogue with the rod. Ellicott: a father comes with a rod (Isaiah 11:4) or in tenderness, as the child’s conduct allows. Adam Clarke: alludes to the teacher and father of verse 15.

The verse closes the section by laying the choice before them. Will they have Paul come with a rod of discipline, or in love and gentleness? The manner of his coming depends on their response.

So the witnesses see Paul end his appeal with a fatherly ultimatum, mingling authority and tenderness. The witnesses note that he offers the Corinthians the choice of how he shall come: with a rod, to chasten their pride and disorder, or in love and meekness, as a father glad to find his children amended. The decision is theirs — let them lay aside their factions and arrogance, and he will come gently; let them persist, and he must come with discipline. They mark how this preserves both his apostolic authority — he does have a rod, and will use it if needed — and his paternal affection, which would far rather come in gentleness. The verse thus brings the long argument to a pointed, personal close: the father has reproved, instructed, and warned; now he asks the children to determine the character of his visit by their repentance. It is a model of pastoral firmness and love, and a final summons to the Corinthians to forsake the pride and division that would otherwise meet the rod.

So the witnesses see Paul end his appeal with a fatherly ultimatum, mingling authority and tenderness. He offers the Corinthians the choice of how he shall come: with a rod, to chasten their pride, or in love and meekness, as a father glad to find his children amended. The decision is theirs — let them lay aside their factions, and he will come gently; let them persist, and he must come with discipline. This preserves both his apostolic authority and his paternal affection. It is a model of pastoral firmness and love, and a final summons to forsake the pride and division that would otherwise meet the rod.
